summary: Gruber frames the post-Google I/O 2010 landscape as an epic iPhone-vs-Android rivalry that benefits consumers through competition. He identifies each platform's distinct strengths — Apple in user experience and design, Google in cloud services and openness — and argues that Google's cloud-to-device capabilities expose Apple's weakness in cloud infrastructure. The piece's sharpest analysis targets Microsoft, arguing they are completely irrelevant in the mobile race: unable to undercut Android's free licensing, lacking momentum, and hemorrhaging market share. Gruber uses Steve Ballmer's infamous 2007 dismissal of the iPhone as a devastating coda, noting that Microsoft's own Windows Mobile share had collapsed to 6.8 percent.
key_points: Apple's mobile strengths are UX, design, and consistency; Google's are cloud services, variety, and permissiveness — each is trying to shift the competition toward their own advantages
iPhone OS devices depend on a PC and iTunes for syncing and backup, which is inherently not 'post-PC,' while Android's cloud-based approach is more forward-looking
Microsoft is the biggest loser: they can't compete on price against free Android licensing, and their volume-dependent business model requires monopoly-scale market share they'll never achieve
Google is playing Microsoft's own game — licensing a platform to many device makers — but doing it for free, making Microsoft's $8-15 per-license model untenable
Ballmer's 2007 prediction of 60-80% Windows Mobile market share aged catastrophically, with actual share dropping to 6.8%
money_quotes: Post-Google I/O, there's not much room left to see iPhone-vs.-Android as anything other than an all-out war.
Competition drives innovation and innovation raises the bar for everyone.
Apple's strengths: user experience, design, consistency. Google's strengths: the cloud, variety, permissiveness.
Relying upon a PC is ipso facto not 'post-PC', and the challenge for Apple is that they've never demonstrated the sort of expertise needed to do this via the cloud.
The big loser this week, though, was Microsoft. They're simply not even part of the game.
Google is playing Microsoft's game — licensing a platform to many device makers.
The big problem for Microsoft is not that there isn't, in theory, room for more than one licensed mobile platform, but rather that Microsoft's model hinges upon monopoly-sized market share.
Not only was he wrong about the iPhone, but he was even more wrong about Windows Mobile.
key_insight: The iPhone-Android rivalry is a healthy competition that raises the bar for consumers, but the real casualty is Microsoft, whose volume-dependent licensing model cannot survive against Android's free alternative.
